lesson.menu_desc: Critical appraisal and hedging: judging a study and questioning it politely -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Questioning Research -- Critical Appraisal & Hedging
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 8 -- Questioning Research

listenings.text: Good morning, everyone, and welcome to this month's journal club. Today I want to walk you through the new study on functional ingredients and blood sugar. Let me start with the strengths. The study design is solid, the design is clear, and the authors describe each step in detail. However, I do have a few queries. First, the cohort is quite small, only forty participants, so the results may not apply to everyone. Second, the study seems to assume that all participants follow the same diet, but that assumption is not really tested. The findings are interesting, and the data suggests a positive effect, but I would say the conclusion is a little strong for the evidence we have. To me, the most important limitation is the short follow-up period. Six weeks is simply not long enough to be confident about long-term effects. So my overall view is that the work is promising and reasonably reliable, but we should treat the headline claim as plausible rather than proven. I would love to hear your thoughts before we move on.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the review committee. I read your appraisal of the functional ingredients study, and I think your comments are very fair. You are right that the cohort is too small to draw a firm conclusion, and I share your reservation about the short follow-up. That said, I would not be too hard on the authors. The study design itself is reliable, and they are quite honest about the limitations in their own discussion. My main query is about possible conflict of interest: the study was funded by the company that makes the ingredient, so we should at least mention that. Overall, I think the findings are plausible, but, like you, I would say the headline is a bit too confident. Let us flag these points politely in our report and ask the authors a few clarifying questions before we make a final decision. Thanks for such a careful review, and talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, summarize what the study set out to show. Next, comment on the strengths of the study design. After that, raise your main query about the cohort or the data. Then, point out the most important limitation. Finally, give a balanced verdict using hedging language.
